agent1_snippets.title: Corpus Callosum Agenesis -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Objectives: Describe the various genetic etiologies of corpus callosum agenesis. Review a full diagnostic workup for agenesis of the corpus callosum, including any diagnostic imaging studies. Summarize the limited options for the management of patients with agenesis of the corpus callosum. Explain possible interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the evaluation and treatment of corpus callosum agenesis and improve outcomes. agent1_snippets.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebral Cortex -- Clinical Significance -- Holoprosencephaly
agent1_snippets.content: In normal gestation, the left and right hemispheres separate between the 5th to 6th week of gestation. Failure of the hemispheres to separate results in a cyclops like-fetus, with fused basal ganglia, a mono ventricle, and a cleft lip/palate. Mutations in the sonic hedgehog signaling pathway, Trisomy 13 and fetal alcohol syndrome have correlations with this condition. [8]

agent1_snippets.content: Treatment is mainly symptomatic and supportive. agent1_snippets.title: Fetal Alcohol Syndrome -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS) is diagnosed by the presence of all of the following criteria: two of the three characteristic facial features (short palpebral fissures, thin vermillion border, and a smooth philtrum), growth retardation (prenatally and/or postnatally), and central nervous system defects. Because all of these criteria are met for diagnosis, fetal alcohol syndrome does not require documentation of prenatal alcohol exposure. Partial fetal alcohol syndrome (pFAS) has two of the characteristic facial features plus, depending on where alcohol exposure was documented, varies in its other criteria. Alcohol-related birth defects (ARBD) is the term used to describe those with physical defects secondary to known fetal alcohol exposure, but who do not have neurobehavioral deficits. On the opposite end of the spectrum, alcohol-related neurodevelopmental disorder (ARND) describes those with neurobehavioral impairment in the setting of documented prenatal alcohol exposure but have minimal to no physical findings and cannot be diagnosed before three years of age. Neurobehavioral disorders associated with prenatal alcohol exposure (ND-PAE) are very similar to alcohol-related congenital disabilities but may involve some physical features.

agent1_snippets.title: Corpus Callosum Agenesis -- Etiology
agent1_snippets.content: Maternal alcohol use during pregnancy Maternal phenylketonuria Chiari II malformation Genetic factors: Agenesis of the corpus callosum often correlates with several aneuploidy and non-aneuploidy syndromes. Chromosomal abnormalities are present in 20% of ACC, especially trisomies 18 and 13. Syndromes that include agenesis of the corpus callosum as an associated feature: Autosomal-dominant - e.g., Apert syndrome and basal cell nevus syndrome, Mowat-Wilson syndrome Autosomal-recessive - e.g., Joubert syndrome and Lyon syndrome X-linked - e.g., Aicardi syndrome and X-linked aqueductal stenosis or hydrocephalus Chromosomal rearrangements - e.g. trisomies, del(4)(p16), del(6)(q23), dup(8)(p21p23), dup(11)(q23qter) Mutations in the disrupted-in-schizophrenia 1 ( DISC1 ) gene located at 1q42.1 have been recently described to be associated with agenesis of the corpus callosum.

agent1_snippets.title: Schizencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
agent1_snippets.content: Schizencephaly is a rare congenital neuronal migration disorder. Type I (closed-lip) can be asymptomatic or diagnosed in adult patients. Type II (open lip) is a severe malformation that can manifest by refractory epilepsy, intellectual disability, and varying degrees of paralysis from hemiparesis to quadriparesis. Possible etiological factors include teratogenic exposures, viral exposures, genetic mutations, and intrauterine fetal stroke. Risk factors of schizencephaly include young maternal age and the illicit use of alcohol and narcotic substances. The diagnostic method of choice for imaging schizencephaly is magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The therapeutic management of both types of schizencephaly is conservative. Surgical treatment is undertaken in some cases with concomitant hydrocephalus or intracranial hypertension.

agent1_snippets.title: Neurosonography Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment -- Congenital Malformations
agent1_snippets.content: Anomalies of the corpus callosum: The corpus callosum (CC) is a midline commissure formed in the 3rd to 4th month of life that connects the two cerebral hemispheres. The corpus callosum consists of four parts that form anterior to posterior: the rostrum, genu, body, and splenium, except for the rostrum, which forms last. Corpus callosum may be completely absent (agenesis) or hypoplastic. Agenesis of the corpus callosum (ACC) can occur as an isolated abnormality or associated with congenital syndromes. [8]

agent1_snippets.title: Fetal Alcohol Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Given that the CNS damage from prenatal alcohol exposure is permanent, there is no cure for fetal alcohol spectrum disorders. However, treatment to mitigate the effects of fetal alcohol spectrum disorders is available. Given the extensive variation in presentation and damage that prenatal exposure to alcohol can cause, treatment for fetal alcohol spectrum disorders is often tailored and specific to individuals. One of the most common treatment approaches is using the medical home to coordinate developmental and educational resources. This treatment modality takes into account the fact that fetal alcohol spectrum disorders disrupt normal neurobehavioral development and that each person can have different manifestations of those disruptions. This treatment methodology seeks to tailor specific therapies to reinforce and address any delays or deficiencies with additional education, practice, and reminders. In summary, when it comes to fetal alcohol spectrum disorders, as is true of most conditions in medicine, the best treatment is prevention. [8] [9] [10] [11]

agent1_snippets.title: Macrocephaly -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Macrocephaly is described as a head circumference more than two standard deviations above the mean for gestational age and sex, which falls above the 97th percentile. Although erroneously used interchangeably with macrocephaly, megalencephaly is a distinct term to suggest increased growth of cerebral structure. Macrocephaly is a broader term to encompass megalencephaly and other causes of increased head size without cerebral overgrowths, such as subdural fluid collection.

agent1_snippets.title: Fetal Alcohol Syndrome -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: Because prenatal alcohol exposure has multiple effects on multiple organ systems, history, and physical findings associated with fetal alcohol spectrum disorders vary widely. In general, diagnoses within fetal alcohol spectrum disorders have one or more of the following features: abnormal facies, central nervous system abnormalities, and growth retardation. However, the different conditions under fetal alcohol spectrum disorders have different diagnostic criteria, and some require documentation of maternal alcohol use during pregnancy.

agent1_snippets.title: Cri Du Chat Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Postnatally
agent1_snippets.content: A diagnosis can be made based on clinical findings. The occurrence of specific characteristic findings such as microcephaly, low birth weight, moon face, muscle hypotonia, and a cat-like cry together should raise clinical suspicion of the condition. Sometimes, this can be difficult because the features may not be obvious as patients show a cytogenetic variation leading to phenotypic variation. The clinical features, as well as the severity and prognosis, can be determined by the size and position of the deletion. If clinical suspicion is present, a karyotype analysis is 1 of the first tests to confirm the diagnosis. However, in cases where the clinical suspicion is high in the presence of a normal karyotype, further specific tests can be carried out, such as fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), comparative genomic hybridization (CGH), or quantitative polymerase chain reaction (PCR). FISH has improved the diagnosis of genetic disorders caused by chromosome deletion and provided a phenotypic map and an individual's genome. Newer techniques, such as CGH, have opened new doors by including the whole genome and the associated markers, which can identify genetic alterations. There have been very few studies on the findings of MRI. However, pontine hypoplasia seems to be the most common feature. This presentation is associated with other findings, such as cerebellar hypoplasia, microcephaly, and corpus callosum anomalies. Supratentorial abnormalities also have been observed. [2] [7]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
agent2_snippets.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

agent2_snippets.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 691
agent2_snippets.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is identified as a specific type of birth trauma. In the context of neonatal and fetal health, birth trauma is mapped to intracerebral bleeding that can occur in the fetus or newborn. Consequently, when a birth trauma event leads to intracerebral hemorrhage, it may result in a cerebral injury that is directly attributable to the traumatic birth process. Understanding this link highlights the importance of recognizing and managing intracerebral bleeding in newborns as a potential cause of cerebral injury stemming from birth trauma.
